original_abstract:
Supervised machine learning typically involves classifying new data points based on a set of previously labeled training examples. In this paper, we demonstrate that the support vector machine (SVM)—a cornerstone of optimized binary classification—can be implemented on a quantum computer with a dramatic reduction in complexity. Specifically, our quantum SVM achieves runtimes that scale logarithmically with both the dimension of the feature vectors and the total number of training examples. 

By achieving this logarithmic scaling, we demonstrate an exponential speedup over classical sampling algorithms in scenarios where they would otherwise require polynomial time. The engine behind this "quantum big data" algorithm is a specialized nonsparse matrix exponentiation technique. This method allows for the efficient execution of matrix inversion on the training data’s inner-product (kernel) matrix, effectively translating high-dimensional optimization problems into a manageable quantum framework.
humanized_abstract:
Most supervised machine learning algorithms work by learning a classification surface between different classes of data, which is fit to the training data to classify new, unseen data points. Given a set of labeled training examples an optimized version of a standard supervised learning algorithm (a Support Vector Machine (SVM) for a binary classification) can be implemented on a quantum computer with significant improvements to computational cost. In particular, we find that the runtime is at most logarithmic in the dimension of the feature space and the number of training examples.

In other words, our new results show that quantum computers can only achieve logarithmic speedup over the best classical sampling algorithms that need polynomial time to analyze even relatively small data sets of exponential size, corresponding to what we call quantum big data. Our fast quantum big data algorithm is based on a new nonsparse matrix exponentiation method for optimal fast matrix inversion of the inner-product (kernel) matrix of the training data, reducing an intricate quantum high-dimensional optimization problem to a relatively simple quantum problem.
